text: asked. “No, we don’t usually get calls about disasters sixty-six years in the past.” Frank and his wife exchanged a puzzled glance, and Frank found himself wondering if the woman was off her rocker. The operator barked a sharp laugh that came through the phone devoid of humor. “The fire at the Phoenix drive-in was in 1956. Worst tragedy this county has ever seen, so many lives lost. The people in this area aren’t likely to forget it.” Frank shook his head. “No, no, you must be mistaken.” “I wasn’t born yet, but I lost a sister in the blaze so my parents certainly never forgot the fire. I heard about it my entire life.” “That’s not possible. We just left there.” “I’ve heard enough of this,” the woman said, biting off her words as if they were bitter in her mouth. “You know it’s a felony to prank call 911, but I’m going to let you off with a warning. You call back, though, and I’ll have your asses in jail.” With that, the line disconnected. “What was she talking about?” Sheryl said softly, her voice trembling. As was her body. “Was she crazy or what?” Frank scooted across the seat and held his wife close to him. “Had to be. She just had to be.” They sat in silence for several moments then Frank slowly turned to look out the back windshield, back toward the Phoenix. There were no flames visible, no churning smoke climbing into the dark sky. Nothing to indicate the blazing hell they’d left behind. Sheryl followed his gaze then grabbed his arm and squeezed hard enough to hurt. “What’s going on? Where’s the fire?” “I don’t know.” Frank sat there for several more minutes before pulling back onto the road, making a U-turn and heading not toward the interstate but back the way they’d come. He expected Sheryl to protest, but she remained silent. Frank almost missed the turn because the sign was no longer there. The dirt road was overgrown with weeds. The ride was bumpier than he remembered, and there was no longer that sense of going through a tunnel because there were considerably less trees. In the glare of the headlights, Frank noted a few blackened stumps. The ticket booth was gone, just some kindling scattered about the ground. Frank rounded the curve into what had been the lot and stopped suddenly. The poles were still there, some of them tilted at crazy angles and almost all of them missing the speakers. Although the screen was no more, the concession stand remained, but it was boarded up, the overhang partially collapsed. Sheryl opened the passenger’s side door and stepped out, wandering away from the car. Frank rolled down his window and stuck his head out. “Honey, get back in the car.” “This can’t be,” Sheryl said, continuing to meander across the lot. “I mean, we were just here. There was a movie playing, there were dozens of people. It was real…wasn’t it?” Reluctantly, Frank got out of the car and started after his wife. “Sheryl, please. We need to go.” Sheryl came to such an abrupt halt Frank almost ran into her back. She bent over, sifting through the dirt and came up with a set of keys that jangled in her hand. “Your keys,” she said without inflection, holding them out to her husband. Absurdly, Frank didn’t want to touch them, as if afraid contact with the keys would send him spiraling into madness. Finally he reached out and allowed Sheryl to drop them into his palm. “Come on, we have to go.” “Frank, aren’t we going to talk about what happened?” “Nothing happened. We are both just tired from the trip and…” “And what? We both had the same hallucination?” “I don’t want to talk about this now. I just want to get home.” “But Frank—” “I said I didn’t want to talk about it!” he shouted, the ferocity in his voice instantly silencing his wife. He didn’t want to talk about it, but he couldn’t stop thinking about it. Specifically how the girl in the ticket booth, the guy at the concession counter, and the “Rebel” who started everything with his cigarette seemed to be aware of their predicament, of being stuck in a repeating loop, which made it worse than any concept of hell Frank could imagine. The “Rebel” had said, “Happens every night.” So if Frank and Sheryl came back tomorrow night, would they get a repeat performance? It was almost too horrific to consider, so Frank just wanted to put it out of his mind. As if that would ever be possible. He took his wife’s hand and they walked quickly back to the car. Without a word, Frank turned the Camry around and started back down the dirt road. He glanced in his rearview mirror just once and looked quickly away. For an instant he thought he’d seen James Dean’s face lit up on a big screen. Kristi DeMeester's storytelling voice is without parallel in today's speculative fiction field. Every story she writes feels intensely personal, cutting with an emotional edge sharp enough to flay your soul, but with such poetry and finesse you never want it to stop. Her short story collection Everything That's Underneath is essential reading, and her first novel, Beneath , is simply one of the most beautifully despairing works I've ever read. I'm certain many readers (including myself) are eagerly looking forward to her second novel, Such A Pretty Smile , coming soon from St. Martin's Press. THE ANIMAL OF OUR BODIES by Kristi DeMeester W e put the candles in the room on a Tuesday afternoon. We close the door. We lock it. You look at me like you did the night of our wedding, all pride and triumph, and I bare my teeth at you in a way that is supposed to be a smile. I am the animal to your soft curves.
